steps.title: Identify the structure
steps.content: Two independent clauses meet at the blank: "Scholars... needn't despair" (IC) and "they can still procure a copy..." (IC). The transition word "though" attaches to the first clause. To join two ICs, you need a semicolon (or period, or comma + FANBOYS conjunction).

steps.title: Apply the rule
steps.content:
"Though" is an adverbial modifier that qualifies "needn't despair" — it attaches to the first clause. A semicolon must then separate the two independent clauses.

A — "despair, though": The comma sets off "though," but then nothing separates the two independent clauses — a comma splice follows. "...despair, though they can still procure..." reads as if "though" subordinates the second clause, reversing the meaning. Fails.

B — "despair, though,": Both commas around "though" treat it as a parenthetical, and then the second independent clause follows with only a comma separating the ICs — a comma splice. Fails.

C — "despair, though;": The comma sets off "though" as a trailing modifier of the first clause; the semicolon then correctly joins the two independent clauses. Correct.

D — "despair—though": An em-dash introduces "though" as if beginning a parenthetical, but no closing em-dash or other punctuation appears before the second clause — the boundary is never resolved. Fails.
